titleStmt.title: Letter from Marie Martin to her mother Mary Martin, 15 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Marie Martin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Marie Helena Martin (1892-1975) to her mother Mary Martin (1866-1955) in Dublin. Marie was a born in Dublin and trained as a nurse at the out break of World War I. Two of Marie's brothers joined the British Army at this time, one of whom was killed in action. Marie served as a nurse in Malta, Leeds and France, and later set up the Medical Missionaries of Mary. Marie states that she has not received much mail lately and that is has been a long time since she has hear from her mother. She discusses her first night on the night shift, stating that it will be difficult. She tells of a meeting she had with a private Martin, a soldier who picked up her brother Charlie's (1895-1915) disc the day before he was killed in action, going on to describe her brothers death. The letter discusses her brother Tommie (1891-?) who is also in the war. Marie tells her mother not to be surprised if they are home in 3 weeks, going on later to account 3 Sisters being sent home for taking a patient out for a drive. She also discusses family members including her aunt Katleen (1874-?) who sent her a lamp.

correspAction.placeName: Greenbank, Monkstown, Dublin, Co. Dublin, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
B1F/1134 15-3-16 My very dearest Mother
It is just ages since we have
had a mail. I have not heard from any
of you for 3 weeks of course I know it
is not your fault. it is just the Mail
has been delayed, we are not looking
forward to the pack one very much it
ought to be a very large one— Many
thanks for cable. I went off that day
to see Pte Martin but missed him as
he was not on pass & as I was not
able to go on Saturday I went on
Sunday & had a long chat with him
he seems a very nice man & has
promised to write to you himself —
I shall tell you exactly what he told
me. I hope you were able to make
something of — He started off
by telling me how he picked up Charlie's
Disc on Dec 7th& as he was not able
to just near Charlie himself that day as
they were advancing he handed it to
Lt. Mahoney. Pte Martin is in A. Company
& was on the left of C, Charlie's Company.
he saw Charlie who was in great form
on the 8th, later on in the day he saw
him hit as all the men kept shouting
keep down Captain Martin or you get hit
& they saw him hit in the leg. Charlie
immediately sat down under cover but
could not get away as the ground was
impossible for him to walk with wound.
Very shortly & through glasses, they saw the
Bulgarians surround where Charlie was
lying & capture the whole trench with
most of C Company in it. Mahony
(of Cork) & White were also capture. the
next thing they saw was was them being
marched Lcyc Bulgarians, he said they
could not actually distinguish any one
but he is quite sure he has not been
killed. If Charlie has be capture
without his disc, it accounts for the
long delay over his name.— I do hope
we shall hear something very soon.
I had a card from Tommy dated March
the 5th from Alexandria, he did not seem
to know what is going to happen to him or
where they are moving on to. They
say here that his division have been
removed from Salonika but like every
thing else it is only a rumour. One thing I am going to tell you now
is not a rumour but very much the
truth. I am on night duty for the
1st time to-day in fact I am sitting
in the bunk now 15-30 writing this.
There are 64 patients but very glad to
say none are bad so it is very light.
I am sure the nights will be very long
with nothing to do. I went to bed
this afternoon but of course could not get
to sleep but I suppose after I have
been on for a couple of nights &
begin to get tired I shall sleep like
an Elephant.
Don't be a bit surprised if you see
us home in about 3 weeks time. this
place is going to close shortly. I
should not be a bit surprised if they
send most of us home. The first lot of
V.A.D.s did go home sometime this week.
Have you heard anything about the
name Malta has got & especially the
V.A.D. we hear it is something trouble.
I have been out here over 4 months & I
do not think I have ever seen any
thing very rough but evidently
some have been making utter fools
of themselves & we have to suffer
for it & be . Two poor unfortunate
sisters are being sent home because
they took a patient out for a drive
the other day. I only know I have had
the quietest & most harmless life out
here sooner than give people the
slightest chance for gossip although I
have be asked many a time to go
out riding which I would have loved
with quite a nice man Captain Pickering
but always refused as it meant
breaking a rule & was not worth
my while to do so. I think it would
nearly be impossible with such a
number of Sisters & Malta such a
a sinah not to find a few who
do not know how to behave themselves
but why we all should suffer nobody
knows. I would have written a few days
ago only I was waiting for the
Mail & have been rather upset as
Miss Paul has suddenly got very ill
so I turned out on the Balcony,
for a night, poor thing. I really do not
know what is rough with her. I hope
she is getting nothing serious.
I do not think I will like night
duty much it is very lonely except
for an occassional visit from a
N.U. & Sisters however I am very lucky
as sister Trower is the night sister
superintendant.
Have you had any late news of
Gerald & did he ask in on
his way back, I have been expect-
ing a letter but of course just like
yours it is still on its way.
Tell A. Kathleen the next time
you see her how useful her torch
is to me now on night duty &
everyone admires it so. I hope you are keeping well & all
at home. I saw A Lily on Sunday
who is in the best of form
& gave me an excellent lunch of
Roast Chicken. fried Pots & peas certainly
they were done well at Floriana
any one that grumbles at the food
there ought to be shot. I heard today
that Floriana is going to be closed.
I wonder what will happen to A. Lily if
that is the case.
I must go and do a round now so
good-night, we will be having our
dinner in half an hour.
Very best of love and kisses to you & all
Your most loving and grateful daughter Marie
